speaker: interviewer
text: Freda, you brought up-- you were telling us that you were now living in Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: In one room.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in one room. And then I-- I had-- I did get me a job in a factory where they make the labels for perfume and for cigarettes. How did I get this job? I knew the owner is a Polish-- from Poland, from Krakow. And I met a girl from Krakow. She said, let's go. Maybe he'll take us. So when I came in, I told him, I said, I don't know Hebrew, nothing, not a word even in Hebrew. But I can do the work. He took us to work. And then I did enroll myself to-- for Hebrew. It was from the [? vitza, ?] next to the big shul in Allenby. They had-- they are not a school, but they had the evening--

speaker: interviewer
text: An ulpan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not ulpan, twice a week. And I used to go for Hebrew, and I used to work in the daytime. And then, you know, it was not easy, absolutely not easy, because you work the whole day, and at night you run for the-- for Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I came for Hebrew, she said, what makes you to marry a Jewish man? I said, because I'm Jewish. She thought as I-- I didn't know nothing, not aleph base, nothing from, you know. What? I said, because I'm Jewish. She said, you're Jewish? You're telling me this right story? I said, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We became very great friends with her. She used to stay in [INAUDIBLE]. I became very big friends with this teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your husband do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband went in a factory. He used to cut leather for this-- for the belts and things like that. And he also got a work through somebody-- you need to know somebody to push in. It was difficult to arrange, but we arranged. We wasn't hungry. We wasn't in the street. We arranged to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I worked in this factory. And I went for the Hebrew. And I knew already a little bit of Hebrew. And I was such a [? pusher. ?] I said you start speaking Hebrew with a lot of-- of mistakes. But I did speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: My friend, from the [HEBREW] she arrived in Israel in-- when-- when the-- already in '41, when the Russians took our part. She went through Vilna. How did it-- a lot of them went through to Israel. Illegal, but they went through. She said, you know what, is a place, [HEBREW] Tel Aviv. It's a [HEBREW], that is, children where they haven't got a home. You see, drunkards-- you know, it was from their parents. And Israel didn't want these children to be with these parents. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: These are children-- problem children who've been removed--

speaker: interviewee
text: Problem children, very problem children.

speaker: interviewer
text: --removed from the parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the parents. And that, you must be a social worker or something to work with these people. But I was like the mother there for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when she-- So did you and your husband then become the houseparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my husband was in his job. And then, I came there. She introduced me to-- from [? Dirya, ?] Tel Aviv, to [? Marburger. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: She introduced me. She say-- he said, I can't take you because she hasn't got social anything-- social-- she's not a social worker. How will she do this job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, [? Pnina, ?] my friend, said to him, she doesn't ask you for money. Just try her. And if it's not good, say goodbye and finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, how can I employ somebody when she needed money? I said to her, how you know-- I was [? 18. I was ?] still cheeky-- I said, how you know I need money? I don't need money. I want the job. I want to work with the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was very successful. And I started working. I was very, very successful.

speaker: interviewer
text: I loved these children-- very big problems. I used to go to [INAUDIBLE] in Tel Aviv and see are they stealing-- one is stealing-- you know, they sell, by the sea they sell cooked-- this-- how do you say? The hot dogs. And I did see are they stealing from there. Falafel?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're stealing the money. One used to go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are your children that you're looking after.

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- but I look after. One did go to school. And in 10 minutes, he used to come. And I did see he's going to the yard and is digging something, putting away his money or something. I used to keep quiet. I used to go and dig out the money to bring it because there was two-- they were social workers. I used to tell them this story, you know, I saw him. She said-- he said, you keep quiet. We will see if he will go for the money again. Very big problems, but lovely children, lovely, absolutely lovely. I used to cry sometimes, you know. I used to cry because I did see as-- you want to help them. And it's-- very difficult it was to help them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hannah [? Aronyah, ?] you did see her on TV. When was she? [INAUDIBLE] She used to come for singing to them all the year-- What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is Shoshanna, Shoshanna [? Tamari. ?] All these, they used to come to entertain the children. They used to help. And I was working there more than a year, with these children [INAUDIBLE]. And then I became a very big shot. They wanted me. And every day I had to sit by another table for breakfast with them, or for lunch, you know. And I was like a mother to them. All the problems that used to come, when we needed something, when somebody needed something-- even [? Dirya, ?] say you're not to listen, and I used to say to do it for me. No, I did that-- don't do it for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're doing it for me. All right? Will you do? They will. They used to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then when I fell pregnant with Batya. I was already six months. And I went to [? Dirya ?] to this. And I said to him, you know, I-- it will be very unpleasant for me to work with all boys, not-- He said, don't worry. You're still going to work. You still will work another month, so, too, you will see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, what is [? Ilya? ?] You say [? 'Ilya.' ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Ilya ?] is like here, the municipality that they did belong to-- the municipality. So I worked till-- I was already really-- I couldn't get anymore. And when Batya was born-- they used to come to visit me, these children. And when Batya was born, and she was lying in a clean bed. And they used to come to me. I've got all the photos from the children. I don't know where to find. I never had such a bed. I never had such a clean thing. They used to tell me that. But I was working still when Batya was born. When Batya was born, I gave up the work, because I knew-- as the doctor told me as I will-- not to fall pregnant again because I had a very tough time, that it [? an ?] [? instrumental ?] child. And he said, just be careful-- not any more children because your life is-- is in danger. And I did want to enjoy her. And my husband did work. And I thought to myself, I did work all from the day what I left home. I will have a little bit of rest and to be with her. And then my brother-in-law made the papers to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: To come where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To here, to South Africa. And he promised us-- that it will be a year or two, we will be able to go back. But there is no back. And then in '53-- end of '53-- we emigrated to South Africa. That was the hardest time for me after the war. When I went to Israel, I took it very easy. I took it easy because, first of all, I was-- I liked the country. I-- I learned the language. I did learn the language. I had friends. I had everything what I needed. And I don't know why I decided to go. I don't know why I decided. My husband said, it will be a year. Let him-- there was five brothers, and only these two was left from his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was your husband's brother that was from South Africa.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's brother, yeah. My sister warned me not to go. And this time, we had already the flat in Tel Aviv. And I-- And I was the queen. I was a queen. I felt so good. I'm in Tel Aviv. I've got friends. I know the language. I don't know what makes me to go. I don't know. I will tell you when I came here, I did struggle a lot. First of all, I didn't know the-- I didn't know one word in English to say, nothing. I was without work. My husband did contracting. I didn't understand. He had a contract, but he had to be in Malmesbury. Don't know Afrikaans, don't know anybody. It must be-- and I'm alone here with a child. And I cannot adjust to the ladies from Cape Town. I could not adjust myself for no money. The best thing was that Hannah Benson was still alive, you know. She had a kindergarten in [? Skunde ?] Street. She died from-- and I met her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, you know what, Freda? You send Batya to the kindergarten. That will be the best thing for her, because she spoke only Hebrew. And that will be the best thing for her and for you. And slowly, I did find a little bit of work. I worked-- my brother-in-law was working with the Rabkin and Hoffman, you know, from the wholesalers. You know, the big grocery? Rabkin, well, he was shot here. He was the communist. You remember the-- Gerald-- Gerald Rabkin's son, David? They had a big house. And they used to make parcels-- Christmas parcels and things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Joan Rabkin-- I met her. And she said, you know, Freda, you-- you will help me. I said, but I don't know English. She said, I will help you. And I start-- slowly, slowly, I did start to work and to meet people. But it was very difficult for me. The adjustment here was very, very difficult. It was a culture-- different culture, different people. The people-- the Jewish people in Cape Town wasn't friendly. They thought that when you come and you-- during-- you went through the war, then you are poison or something, I don't know. The attitude wasn't a nice attitude at all. But you know, slowly you get to know-- they get to know you. But still, very difficult-- it was very difficult for me. [DRINKS] Then Batya starts school when Mr. Ketz was still the principal. She started [? Heltzlier ?] school. And he knew I-- I struggle a lot. And so he called me. And he speaks with me Hebrew. And he says to me in Hebrew, Mr. Ketz-- Freda, let me enroll Batya and don't pay. I said, Mr. Ketz, if I mustn't pay for Batya, then she will go in a government school. And she will speak with me Hebrew at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, he said, why? I said, because I don't like charity. I went through my whole life without asking anybody for help. So I will not now let-- let myself go because I came to this rich, golden country, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he said, then I will give you the maximum of how much you must pay. I said, fine. And then Batya started school. Then I took-- [? a fish ?] [? shop ?] I took in [? Seaport. ?] I didn't know what it is because I had 800 pounds. But my husband and me, we did save every penny. Maybe we'll go something. I bought this [? fish ?] [? shop ?] from a German, from a German officer. I bought it. And I started working. I started working.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I came to order something, they ask me, do you know something? I said, no. They said, why did you buy it? It's such a difficult business.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I can't help it. I bought it already. I do have to do it. There was an English man, and he did help me and two colored boys. And I did work in this fish shop still. Pekim and Pekim-- there was this-- another firm. And it started to be very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because I came one Friday home, And I start crying, my husband said, what happened to you? I said, I can't manage. That is impossible for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't say a word. Next Friday, he came with one from Malmesbury-- what he did work there-- a farmer. And he said, we are going to the shop. And we will show it to you. He doesn't want the shop. But he wants all the fridges and everything. And I will-- he said, Mrs. Glezer, I'm going to buy all these things and the goods that you have, everything. Because you can't throw it out in the street. We will buy it from you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had to write everybody a letter to thank me because a lot of people was-- like the Bloombergs, or Mrs. Sachs, Irene Sachs, and Mrs. Gerald Climons-- they was always coming to me. I was always getting presents from them. They was a different class of people. When I wrote them the letter, they said, why? Freda, why? You haven't got money for this. And I haven't got the strength anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't do it anymore. I must sell. I didn't sell for-- I sell all the equipment. And I went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I went to Israel for six, seven weeks after this-- holiday-- and I came back. I came back, and I went to-- for a job in [? Trasbeng ?] was a-- what is this? I forget-- Markman, a shop from clothes, a lot of shops. And I was working there, still even to Australia. And the Suez was closed. You remember the story when the Suez Canal was closed? The Suez Canal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was closed. And all the immigrants had to come through-- [INAUDIBLE] through this routine. And they used to pay him with dollars, with-- you know, all these. I used to follow him and say, Mr. [? Markman, ?] you must come. I don't-- he said, go the bank. Ask what is the [? routine? ?] He took suitcases of money from [? Iran. ?] Then, when he when away, I went also again to Israel for a holiday. And I came back. And I start [? MidEast ?] shop that I'm working still now. That's my story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you got some photographs now that you can show us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewer
text: Of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only this, what I did take it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You wanted to see it? I have a lot of photos, but that doesn't interest you. That is me and my husband and Batya. That is Ida, her name. And she was-- the photo was taken in 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is Ida?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was killed. I don't how she was killed. But she was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was she killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the-- I don't know, you see. We-- I didn't find anybody, no witnesses, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Germans came, she was killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she was-- she was alive. When I left, she was alive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: A beautiful woman-- the most beautiful girl. And was she a school girl when that was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was-- finished matric. She finished matric in Russia, she and the other sister. It was a brilliant person.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is Luba, my friend from [? Lubna. ?] We went through together the whole war. We came together to Israel. And she was killed. After three years being [? Kinesha, ?] she was killed by a bus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she in Namangan with you also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, from the day that we left together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the goat shed and everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everywhere. I've got her last letter. I treasure this last letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- when you were in [? Wenzlow? ?] In [? Wenzlow, ?] Yeah. And this [? man ?] [? also ?] says-- Is she the one who worked with you in the camp--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, everywhere we was together. You could not take one and another not. We were just like sisters. They knew-- they knew that, if they will take me, and they will not take her, she will not do it. See, we must be together. We were all over together. She went to study social work in Israel. I sent her books. I've got even the receipt somewhere. And she was already killed. She didn't receive the books. Very clever, very intelligent person. Miriam [? Merteman ?] met her in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this a photograph of you in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [? Wenzlow. ?] [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: That is me taken in [? Wenzlow ?] In the DP camps in '46-- in 1946. What else do you want? What are the buildings in the back?

speaker: interviewee
text: The buildings are the barracks where we used to stay. And we were accommodated five people in a room. And that really was a luxury. For me, it was a luxury.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm wearing a coat made from a blanket from the-- from the DP. We used to get blankets, green [? bay ?] blankets. So I made a coat from them. That is my favorite I'm wearing. And I was very cold then. But I'm so [? decked ?] out because I was very cold. It was winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany winter is freezing. It's 18 degrees, 17 degrees.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is me, my husband, and my daughter, Batya. The picture was taken 10 years ago in Cape Town. I don't remember what occasion it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your husband pass away?

speaker: interviewee
text: 16 Years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: 16 Years ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then the picture must have been taken more than 10 years ago then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, must be more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it probably was 20 years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: 20 Years, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does Batya do now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Batya is introducing medical books. She's working for [? Meskin ?] Miller. Her-- all the-- the last 15 years she works only for medical books.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where does she stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Johannesburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my daughter, Batya. And I don't remember if I took in Israel or in Cape Town, this picture. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that all?
